--- Page 1 ---
R-equivalence on Cubic Surfaces I:
Existing Cases with Non-Trivial Universal Equivalence
Dimitri Kanevsky, Julian Salazar, and Matt Harvey
Google DeepMind, 1600 Amphitheatre Parkway, Mountain View, CA 94043
E-mail: {dkanevsky,julsal,mattharvey}@google.com
Abstract
Let V be a smooth cubic surface over a p-adic field k with good reduction. Swinnerton-Dyer (1981)
proved that R-equivalence is trivial on V (k) except perhaps if V is one of three special types—those
whose R-equivalence he could not bound by proving the universal (admissible) equivalence is trivial.
We consider all surfaces V currently known to have non-trivial universal equivalence. Beyond being
intractable to Swinnerton-Dyer’s approach, we observe that if these surfaces also had non-trivial R-
equivalence, they would contradict Colliot-Th´el`ene and Sansuc’s conjecture regarding the k-rationality
of universal torsors for geometrically rational surfaces.
By devising new methods to study R-equivalence, we prove that for 2-adic surfaces with all-Eckardt
reductions (the third special type, which contains every existing case of non-trivial universal equiv-
alence), R-equivalence is trivial or of exponent 2.
For the explicit cases, we confirm triviality: the
diagonal cubic X3 + Y 3 + Z3 + ζ3T 3 = 0 over Q2(ζ3)—answering a long-standing question of Manin’s
(Cubic Forms, 1972)—and the cubic with universal equivalence of exponent 2 (Kanevsky, 1982).
This is the first in a series of works derived from a year of interactions with generative AI models
such as AlphaEvolve and Gemini 3 Deep Think, with the latter proving many of our lemmas.
We
disclose the timeline and nature of their use towards this paper, and describe our broader AI-assisted
research program in a companion report (in preparation).
1
Introduction
Let V (k) be the set of geometric k-points of a geometrically irreducible and reduced projective
variety V . R-equivalence is the relation on V (k) where P ∼Q if they are connected by some
chain of rational curves in V , i.e., there exists a finite sequence of k-points P, . . . , Q where each
adjacent pair lies in the image of a k-morphism from P1
k to V .
It is pertinent to Diophantine
problems—in other words, to describing the set of points V (K) where K is a number field—as it
is the finest equivalence relation such that two points given by the same parametric solution to
a system of equations defining V are equivalent [SD04]. For example, because the Brauer-Manin
pairing is constant under R-equivalence, this relation has been used to prove the sufficiency of the
Brauer-Manin obstruction to the Hasse principle [Man86, §VI] and to weak approximation [SD01]
for certain varieties. However, due to the challenge of determining the existence of chains of rational
curves, R-equivalence has historically resisted computation.
Despite this difficulty, cubic hypersurfaces V offer a tractable setting for arithmetic geometry
due to the additional algebraic structure of their k-points. When dim V = 1, one retrieves the rich
study of points on elliptic curves and their abelian group structure, which expresses geometrically
1
arXiv:2603.19215v1  [math.AG]  19 Mar 2026


--- Page 2 ---
as the chord-and-tangent process. However, applying this geometric process to dim V > 1 does not
give a well-defined binary operation due to e.g., the presence of lines on V . Manin [Man68] pro-
posed to modulo out this difficulty, deriving the study of admissible point classes on cubic surfaces
and their commutative Moufang loop (CML) structure (Section 2), which can be non-associative
[Kan23]. Thus for cubic hypersurfaces, proving R-equivalence is admissible guarantees it has a
CML structure; then, computing the universal admissible equivalence provides an upper bound.
This motivation, combined with Manin’s geometric formulation of admissibility, led Swinnerton-
Dyer [SD81] to investigate this finer universal equivalence for smooth cubic surfaces V over local
fields k with good reduction—i.e., where the surface is defined by a cubic equation with integer
coefficients whose reduction modulo p defines a smooth cubic surface—by lifting where possible
from their reductions over finite fields. In this way he showed all points in V (k) are universally
equivalent and thus R-equivalent, except possibly when the characteristic of the residue field ˜k is
2 or 3 and conditions on the special fiber ˜V (i.e., V ’s reduction mod p) are exceptional; we restate
his Theorems 1 and 2, for finite and local fields respectively, in Section 2.3.
This first work considers the possibility of non-trivial R-equivalence arising from the cases
currently known to have non-trivial universal equivalence [Kan82]. The interest in computing R-
equivalence for these surfaces stems from their unique position in the arithmetic landscape: because
these surfaces possess smooth reduction, one expects their Brauer equivalence—where two points
are equivalent if they give the same output under the Brauer-Manin pairing for all elements of the
Brauer group Br(X)—to be trivial. More precisely, let S be the N´eron-Severi torus of X. For
any smooth, projective, geometrically rational surface X over a characteristic 0 field k with a k-
point, the map A0(X) →H1(k, S) is injective [CT83, Prop.4]; furthermore, in the case of smooth
reduction over a p-adic field, this map is zero [Blo81, Thm.0.2] [Dal05, Thm.4] and therefore A0(X)
is trivial. The Brauer-Manin pairing respects rational equivalence, so this implies trivial Brauer
equivalence. This creates a compelling opening: if R-equivalence were non-trivial in these cases, it
would be strictly finer than rational and thus Brauer equivalence. This would give the first example
of a smooth, projective, geometrically rational surface X over a field of characteristic zero where
the map X(k)/R →CH0(X) is not injective. Such a result would be particularly noteworthy as it
relates to a long-standing question posed by Colliot-Th´el`ene and Sansuc: are universal torsors over
such surfaces k-rational as soon as they possess a k-point [CTS87, §2.8]? If so, the descent map
X(k)/R →H1(k, S), which factors through CH0(X), must be injective—a contradiction.
While we ultimately do not find non-trivial R-equivalence in this work—consistent with the con-
jecture—testing these boundaries remains a primary motivation for the new R-equivalence methods
developed here. Furthermore, resolving R-equivalence for these cases is compelling in its own right,
as they represent a gap in our methods to compute R-equivalence and exceptions to what would
otherwise be a clean statement on cubic surfaces over local fields (Theorem 2.15). In particular,
Manin [Man72, end of §II.6] asked about the universal and R-equivalence of the projective cubic
V : X3 + Y 3 + Z3 + θT 3 = 0
over Q2(θ), where θ2 + θ + 1 = 0,
(1)
i.e., θ is a primitive third root of unity (ζ3). While its universal equivalence was established as
Z/3 × Z/3 early on [Kan82, 1.6], its R-equivalence has remained open [SD81, Thm.2.iii] [Man86,
§16] [SD01, §6] despite advances in non-constructive determination of R-equivalence (Section 1.2).
2


--- Page 3 ---
1.1
Main Results
First, we outline currently known situations of smooth cubic surfaces V over p-adic field k with
good reduction that guarantee non-trivial universal equivalence. Note that these fall under case
(iii) of [SD81, Thm. 2] (Theorem 2.15):
1. ˜V (˜k) is line-free and all-Eckardt, with more than one point [Kan82, Cor. 2.5]. Equation (1)
is the explicit case of this [Man72, Ex. II.6.5] [Kan82, Ex. 1.6.i].
2. V : F1,1,1(T) = 0 from Equation (2). This is a case of ˜V (˜k) line-free and a single (Eckardt)
point [Kan82, §3.4], which allows but does not guarantee non-trivial equivalence.
Fb0,b1,b2(T) = T 2
0 T1 + T0T 2
1 + T 3
2 + T 2
2 T3 + T 3
3 + T1(T 2
1 + T2T3 + T 2
2 + T 2
3 )
+ 2T0(b0T 2
2 + b1T2T3 + b2T 2
3 ).
(2)
In Section 3, we prove the following general result that covers both cases:
Theorem A. Let V be a smooth cubic surface over a 2-adic local field k with good reduction such
that points ˜V (˜k) ̸= ∅are all Eckardt. Then R-equivalence on V (k) is trivial or of exponent 2.
Its proof primarily relies on the application of Manin’s norm map for quadratic extensions. This
map preserves the period-three components within the loop of universal equivalence classes on the
surface. From it, we can easily specialize to the broad first case:
Corollary 1.1. In addition, if ˜V (˜k) is line-free, is comprised of more than one Eckardt point, and
V (k) contains an Eckardt point, then R-equivalence is trivial. In particular, V : X3 + Y 3 + Z3 +
ζ3T 3 = 0 over Q2(ζ3) has trivial R-equivalence.
Proof. We meet the conditions of [Kan82, Cor. 1.5], giving universal equivalence that is of exponent
3. R-equivalence is coarser, so it cannot be exponent 2. Therefore it must be trivial. One can verify
that Manin’s diagonal cubic meets all conditions; see [SD81, §8] and [Kan82, Ex. 1.6.i].
However, Manin’s original norm construction [Man86, §15] is not applicable to the period-two
components of this loop.
In Section 4, we extend the method to encompass these period-two
components.
Specifically, we consider a specialization of the other published 2-adic case, from
[Kan82, 3.4], where universal equivalence is known to be exponent two. Let V be the cubic surface
over Q2 defined by Fb0,b1,b2 = 0 from Equation (2) where bi = 1 mod 2 for all i (slightly generalizing
the second definitive case). Modulo 2 it gives the same surface with one point over F2 that was
considered in [SD81]). The work demonstrated an admissible equivalence on V (Q2) with a non-
trivial period-two component, giving a lower bound on the structure of universal equivalence. Using
our extended method, we explicitly compute the universal and R-equivalence of this example:
Theorem B. Let V defined by Equation (2) be smooth such that ˜V has exactly one point. Then
V (Q2) constitutes a single class of R-equivalence and two classes of universal equivalence.
In all, we use new and expanded methods to resolve two long-standing cases of exception (iii) to
[SD81]’s Theorem 2 (reproduced as Theorem 2.15).
Finally, in Section 5 we disclose the non-trivial role played by generative artificial intelligence
(AI) in completing this work.
Some distinctive aspects as of March 2026 include the relative
3


--- Page 4 ---
notability of the problem (Section 1.2), reliance on literature and methods largely unavailable
online, and the unexpected role of AI in improving rigor in a domain that tends towards high-level
geometric reasoning. We also include a timeline of AI use, occurring over a year across multiple
models—longer than other AI-assisted results—glimpsing how AI plays into the messiness of long-
term research.
Finally, our background is atypical: instead of being full-time mathematicians
coordinating with AI researchers, we are all career AI researchers who were full-time mathematicians
9+ years ago (though the first author, a student of Manin’s, has continued to publish in the field
[Kan23, Kan25]).
Remark 1.2. This is only the first publication atop a growing body of theory and results over a
year (thus far). We propose and discuss this paradigm of AI-assisted mathematical theory building
in a forthcoming companion report [SKH+26].
Acknowledgements: We thank Jean-Louis Colliot-Th´el`ene for his correspondence on this subject.
We extend our gratitude to our colleagues at Google DeepMind, particularly Lucas Dixon for his
encouragement and assistance in utilizing these systems, Daniel Zheng for his helpful comments
that improved the paper’s content, Adam Zsolt Wagner for onboarding us to AlphaEvolve, and
Martin Wattenberg, Eric Wieser, and many others who provided useful discussions and shared
promising mathematical tools and agents we hope to leverage in future work.
1.2
Timeline of Prior Work
We give a brief history of R-equivalence on p-adic cubic hypersurfaces, and the question of excep-
tional cases, for generalist readers:
• 1968: The theory of CMLs of admissible equivalence classes on cubic hypersurfaces, gener-
alizing the chord-tangent construction of elliptic curves, is introduced by Manin [Man68].
• 1972: Manin uses this theory to prove R-equivalence is finite for smooth cubic hypersurfaces
over local fields. The cubic surface of Theorem 1.1 is shown to have a non-trivial admissible
equivalence over the 2-adics by Manin, who then asks about its R-equivalence [Man72, §II.6].
• 1981: Swinnerton-Dyer [SD81] shows R-equivalence is trivial for many p-adic cubic surfaces
with good reduction by bounding them via their trivial universal admissible equivalence. How-
ever, as Manin’s exceptional surface has a non-trivial admissible equivalence, it is not covered
by [SD81]’s methods, falling into exception (iii) of Theorem 2 (in general, the exceptions occur
in p = 2, 3). [SD81, §8] references Manin and his surface and resolves R-equivalence for the
finite-field reduction of the surface, but not the surface itself.
• 1982: Kanevsky [Kan82] proves that Manin’s admissible equivalence is universal and shows
another exception (iii) to Theorem 2—the one-point reduction case—has a non-trivial admis-
sible equivalence.
• 1984: Kanevsky’s preprint [Kan84] claims to show Manin’s surface has trivial R-equivalence.
• 1986: [Man86] acknowledges some results from [Kan84] but not its proof of R-equivalence
for his surface, restating the question as open.
4


--- Page 5 ---
• 1987: In their treatise [CTS87] on the theory of descent on rational varieties, Colliot-Th´el`ene
and Sansuc note their theory’s reliance on “la premi`ere hypoth`ese sur les torseurs universels,”
for which no smooth, projective, k-rational counterexamples exist. Weaker versions of the
hypothesis are known to fail, but not for surfaces.
• 1999: Using deformation theory, Kollar [Kol99] proved that R-equivalence is finite for ratio-
nally connected varieties over local fields, generalizing Manin’s finiteness result via different
means.
This result was published in Annals (the top journal in mathematics, which per
[FTB+26]’s human-AI taxonomy is an indicator of a Level 3 “Major Advance”), to help
readers place the historical significance of this line of work.
• 2001: Swinnerton-Dyer [SD01, §6] relates R-equivalence to the long-standing question of
weak approximation (i.e., when a cubic surface over the p-adics approximates its points over
a number field). However, he notes his methods still cannot lift his R-equivalence proof for
the finite-field reduction to compute R-equivalence for Manin’s surface.
• 2003, 2008: New results prove trivial R-equivalence on more p-adic cubic hypersurfaces.
However, these results are for hypersurfaces with large residue fields [KS03] or in higher
dimensions [Mad03, Mad08], due to the limitations of e.g., deformation-theoretic approaches.
2
Background
In general V will denote a geometrically irreducible and reduced projective cubic hypersurface
defined over a field k. As many of the statements below are in publications not easily accessible,
we summarize the known results needed here.
2.1
Algebraic Structures on Cubic Hypersurfaces
Here we follow the approach first introduced in [Man68] and refined in [Man86]:
Definition 2.1. Three points P1, P2, P3 ∈V (k) are collinear if they lie on a straight line L
(as understood in a projective space containing V ) that is defined over k, and either L ⊂V or
P1 + P2 + P3 is the intersection cycle V · L.
Hence, for any P1, P2 ∈V (k) there exists P3 ∈V (k) such that P1, P2, P3 are collinear, and
if P1 ̸= P2 and there is no straight line on V defined over k through P1, P2, then P3 is uniquely
defined. For such pairs of points we can define P1 ◦P2 := P3.
Definition 2.2. An equivalence relation A on Vr(k) (which denotes non-singular points of V (k))
is said to be admissible if when P1, P2, P3 and P ′
1, P ′
2, P ′
3 are collinear triples such that P1 ∼A P ′
1
and P2 ∼A P ′
2, then P3 ∼A P ′
3.
Admissible relations are those which turn collinearity into a binary operation on cubic hypersur-
faces. If [P] is the equivalence class of P in Vr(k)/A, where A is an admissible equivalence relation,
then the operation [P1] ◦[P2] is well-defined and can be characterized as follows:
Fact 2.3 (Collinearity as binary operation).
5


--- Page 6 ---
• If P1 ̸= P2 and there is no line through these points lying on V , then P1 ◦P2 is uniquely
determined as the third point of intersection of the line ⟨P1, P2⟩with V . Its class is denoted
[P1 ◦P2].
• If P1 and P2 lie on a line L belonging to V , then admissibility necessitates that L ∩Vr(k)
collapses into a single class; thus, we define [P1] ◦[P2] := [P1] = [P2].
• If P1 = P2 and there is no line through P1 lying on V over k, let C be the tangent section (the
intersection of V with the tangent plane at P1). Then all points on C distinct from P1 belong
to the same admissible equivalence class. Furthermore, if C possesses a rational tangent line
at P1, then all points on C (including P1 itself) belong to the same admissible equivalence
class.
Proposition 2.4. For admissible A and any S1, S2 ∈Vr(k)/A, there is a uniquely determined class
S3 ∈Vr(k)/A such that there exist collinear points P1, P2, P3 with Pi ∈Si. By defining a new binary
operation S1S2 := [O]◦(S1 ◦S2) where O is a fixed point, one converts Vr(k)/A into a commutative
Moufang loop (CML) with [O] as the identity.
CMLs are a non-associative generalization of abelian groups. For brevity, we write
MA(k) := CML(Vr(k)/A, [O])
for admissible A, as V is implicit and the CML structure is independent of the base point [O]
[Man86, 5.1], generalizing the result for the geometric group law on elliptic curves.
Remark 2.5. We write “A-equivalence” to refer to either the equivalence relation A, or the (iso-
morphism class of) CMLs derived from admissible A, based on context.
Finally, we can take the initial object of all such admissible quotients:
Definition 2.6. Universal equivalence is the finest admissible equivalence relation on Vr(k).
Proposition 2.7. The universal equivalence exists and is unique, with all admissible relations being
quotients of it. When [O] is fixed, there is a natural surjection of CMLs (MU(k) →MA(k)).
2.2
Properties of R-equivalence for dim V ≥2
We use the definition of R-equivalence that we gave at the beginning of the introduction sec-
tion. One can find some different equivalent definitions of R-equivalence in [Man86, 14.1-2]. R-
equivalence is defined for any algebraic variety V over a field k. However, additional algebraic
structure comes under very mild cubic hypersurface conditions on V .
Theorem 2.8 ([Kol02, Thm. 1]). Let V be smooth with dim V ≥2. Then V (k) ̸= ∅(i.e., V
contains a k-point) if and only if V is unirational over k, i.e., there exists a dominant rational map
f : Pn
k 99K V defined over k.
Remark 2.9. The condition “k-point of general type” was almost exclusively used by Manin to
establish k-unirationality via [Man86, Thm.12.11].
Likewise, Kanevsky only uses the existence
of a k-point of general type to use one of Manin’s unirationality results, namely that admissible
equivalence classes are open in the k-topology for local fields k [Kan82, 2.7]. Hence, via Theorem 2.8
we can drop the “general type” part moving forward, citing this remark when we have done so.
6


--- Page 7 ---
Proposition 2.10 ([Man86, Thm. 14.3]). If V is unirational and k is an infinite field, then R-
equivalence on Vr(k) is admissible.
Recall that admissible equivalences collapse lines in V into classes; hence, so does the univer-
sal admissible equivalence. Intuitively, R-equivalence collapses rational curves in V into classes,
resulting in a coarser admissible relation.
In the case of cubic surfaces and higher-dimensional cubic hypersurfaces, the algebraic structure
induced via collinearity as a binary operation is more restricted than in elliptic curves.
Proposition 2.11 ([Man86, Cor. 13.3] and Theorem 2.9). Let A be admissible on Vr(k) ̸= ∅. If the
field k is infinite and dim V ≥2, then MA(k) is the direct product of an abelian group of exponent
2 and a CML of exponent 3.
2.3
Results from [SD81]
Let ˜k = Ok/pk denote the residue field of k (in general, a tilde denotes reduction mod pk).
Definition 2.12. A (geometric) Eckardt point defined over k on a cubic surface V is a point
P ∈V (k) where V · TP V decomposes into three straight lines through P in the algebraic closure ¯k.
Here, we reproduce the key results and intermediate findings of [SD81] in our notation.
Definition 2.13. The normalized Hessian H∗of a cubic form F(X, Y, Z, T) = 0 is given by
(1.1)
H∗(X, Y, Z, T) = 1
4

FXX
FXY
FXZ
FXT
FY X
FY Y
FY Z
FY T
FZX
FZY
FZZ
FZT
FTX
FTY
FTZ
FTT

where the subscripts denote derivatives.
Theorem 2.14 ([SD81, Thm. 1]). Let V be a nonsingular cubic surface defined over the finite field
of q elements, and suppose that V contains n rational points; then all these points belong to the
same class for universal equivalence except when V contains no rational line and all its rational
points are Eckardt. In the exceptional case there are n classes each consisting of one point; and
either q = 2, n = 3 or q = 4, n = 9.
Theorem 2.15 ([SD81, Thm. 2]). Let V be a nonsingular cubic surface with equation
F(X, Y, Z, T) = 0,
where the coefficients of F are integers in a p-adic field k; then ˜V is given by ˜F = 0 and we assume
that this equation defines a nonsingular cubic surface (good reduction). All the rational points of
V belong to the same class for universal equivalence except perhaps in the following three cases:
(i) char ˜k = 3 and the surface ˜H∗= 0 touches ˜V at every rational point of ˜V .
(ii) char ˜k = 2 and ˜H∗vanishes.
(iii) char ˜k = 2 and every rational point of ˜V is an Eckardt point.
In particular [SD81, §5] gives an explicit list of representatives for reductions satisfying (iii).
Lemma 2.16 ([SD81, Lem. 14]). Let ˜P1, ˜P2, ˜P3 ∈˜V defined over ˜k be collinear; they can be lifted
to points P1, P2, P3 ∈V defined over k which are collinear in the same sense.
7


--- Page 8 ---
2.4
Results under field extensions
We now describe the behaviour of R-equivalence classes Vr(k)/R under a quadratic extension K of
the base field, where there exists a norm map that passes from K to k:
Proposition 2.17 ([Man86, Prop. 15.1-15.1.1]). Let V be unirational and [K : k] = 2 where K is
separable over an infinite field k. Let i : Vr(k)/R →Vr(K)/R be the morphism mapping classes
to classes. Then there exists a map of sets N : Vr(K)/R →Vr(k)/R such that N(i(S)) = S ◦S.
Furthermore, viewing i as a CML morphism:
S ∈ker(i) =⇒S2 = [O] ◦(S ◦S) = [O] ◦N(i(S)) = [O] ◦N(i([O])) = [O] ◦([O] ◦[O]) = [O],
i.e., the kernel of i only contains elements of order 2.
[Kan84, Prop. 5.1] implicitly turns Theorem 2.11 into a specialized version of the following
structure result:
Proposition 2.18. Let V be unirational with dim V ≥2 over an infinite field k. Let K be a tower
of separable quadratic extensions over k. Then:
#MR(k)[3] ≤#MR(K)[3] ≤#MU(K)[3]
(3)
where U is the universal equivalence relation and [3] denotes the 3-torsion component of the CML.
Proof. Let S be a class of points on a cubic surface V (k) belonging to the 3-torsion component
of the associated CML, i.e., S ∈MR(k)[3]. This is a CML of exponent 3. Let ℓ/k be the first
separable quadratic extension; by Theorem 2.17, the CML morphism i : Vr(k)/R →Vr(ℓ)/R is
injective. Hence #MR(k)[3] ≤#MR(ℓ)[3]; repeating this reasoning gives ≤#MR(K)[3]. Then,
by Theorem 2.10, R-equivalence is admissible on Vr(K), making it compatible but coarser than
universal (admissible) equivalence, and so we have a surjection Vr(K)/U →Vr(K)/R of CMLs. It
follows that #MU(K)[3] ≥#MR(K)[3].
2.5
Lifting Results on dim V = 2
The primary strategy for equivalence results on local fields has involved describing under which
conditions equivalence on finite fields tells us about their local fields.
Definition 2.19. We say that P1, P2 are in general position when P1 ̸= P2 and the line P1P2 is
neither tangent to V nor contained in V .
The following facts follow from theorems 11.7, 13.2 and corollaries 6.1.3, 13.3 in [Man86].
The universal equivalence U on V (k) can be split into two admissible equivalences U2 and U3 such
that U = U2 ∩U3, where |V (k)/U3| = 3n and |V (k)/U2| = 2m for non-negative integers n, m. These
split equivalences satisfy the following properties:
• Property 3 (U3): For any class X ∈V (k)/U3, we have X ◦X = X.
• Property 2 (U2): There is a class X0 ∈V (k)/U2 such that for any class X ∈V (k)/U2, we
have X ◦X = X0.
8


--- Page 9 ---
Because U is the overall finest admissible equivalence, Ui (for i ∈{2, 3}) is strictly the finest
admissible equivalence on V (k) satisfying the respective Property i. We denote the corresponding
finest admissible equivalence satisfying Property i on ˜V (˜k) by ˜Ui.
Definition 2.20 (i-Class Free Point). Let i ∈{2, 3}. A point ˜P ∈˜V (˜k) is called i-class free if all
points on V (k) lifted from ˜P belong to the exact same class Ui.
Then:
Proposition 2.21 ([Kan82, Prop. 2.4] and Theorem 2.9). Let V (k) ̸= ∅be a nonsingular cubic
surface defined over a p-adic local field k. Suppose upon reduction to the residue field ˜k that no
straight lines defined over ˜k and lying on ˜V pass through its Eckardt points. For an Eckardt point
˜P ∈˜V (˜k) let E ˜P denote equivalence classes of points P reducing to ˜P. If an Eckardt point ˜P is in
general position together with some point of ˜V (˜k), then ˜P is class-free for char ˜k ̸= 2, 3, CML(E ˜P )
has period 2 for char ˜k = 2, and CML(E ˜P ) has period 3 for char ˜k = 3.
3
Proof of Theorem A
First we prove a few lemmas.
Lemma 3.1. Let K be a local field with ring of integers OK and residue field k. Let V ⊂P3
K be a
cubic surface defined by a homogeneous polynomial F. Assume that V has good reduction, meaning
the reduction ˜V is smooth over k.
Then, every line ˜L on ˜V lifts uniquely to a line L on V .
Proof. It is a standard result that the Fano scheme of lines on a smooth cubic surface is smooth of
dimension 0. As noted by Swinnerton-Dyer in the proof of Lemma 14, [SD81], this implies that the
system of equations defining the lines has a non-vanishing Jacobian determinant at any solution
˜L on the smooth reduction ˜V . Consequently, by the multivariate Hensel’s Lemma, the solution ˜L
lifts uniquely to a line L on V defined over OK.
Lemma 3.2 ([Man86, Prop. 13.7]). Let V be a smooth cubic surface over k. If V contains a k-
rational line, then its universal equivalence group MU(k) is 3-torsion-free (i.e., MU(k)[3] ∼= {1}).
Proof. Let L be a k-rational line on V . Since universal equivalence is admissible, all points in
L(k) belong to the same universal equivalence class, which we may denote as the identity class [e].
Now, let P ∈V (k) be a point not on L and let [P] denote its U3 equivalence class. There exists a
k-rational plane Π containing L and P. The intersection of this plane with V consists of the line
L and a residual conic C passing through P.
Let P0 be a k-rational intersection point of L and the tangent line to C at P (which belongs to
Π). Since (P, P, P0) are collinear, [P] ◦[P] = [P0] = [e]. In the context of the U3 equivalence, this
implies [P] ◦[P] = [P] = [e]. Since this holds for an arbitrary point P, it follows that the 3-torsion
is trivial.
We will also use the following generalized Hasse-Weil bound, due to Aubry and Perret [AP96]
and concurrently Leep and Yeomans [LY94]:
9


--- Page 10 ---
Proposition 3.3 (Hasse-Weil for singular curves). Let X be an absolutely irreducible projective
curve over FQ with arithmetic genus πX. Then:
|#X(FQ) −(Q + 1)| ≤2πX
p
Q
Since πX accounts for the singularities (specifically πX = ggeom+δ, where δ measures singularity
complexity), this bound holds regardless of smoothness.
Lemma 3.4. Let V ⊂P3 be a smooth cubic surface defined over ˜k. For any integer n ≥1, there
exists a finite tower of quadratic extensions ˜K/˜k such that for any set of n points P1, . . . , Pn ∈
V( ˜K), one can find a point P ′ ∈V( ˜K) in general position with respect to P.
Proof. We split the proof into a few steps, first establishing the case of n = 1, writing P for P1,
then unifying over n points.
Step 1: Geometric Setup
Fix a point P ∈V( ˜K). We identify the set of points P ′ ∈V that are not in general position
with P. Let F(X) = 0 be the equation of V. The intersection of the line LP,P ′ with V leads to
the condition for the “bad locus” BP :
BP = CP ∪DP
where:
1. CP = V ∩TP V: The intersection of the surface with the tangent plane at P. Since V is a
smooth surface, the Zariski tangent space TP V is strictly a 2-dimensional plane, making CP
a plane cubic curve (degree 3).
2. DP = V ∩{Q | ∆Q(P) = 0}: The intersection of the surface with the first polar quadric of
P. Because V is absolutely irreducible, this forms a proper space curve of degree 2 × 3 = 6.
The total degree of the bad locus is deg(BP ) = 9.
Step 2: Point Counting Bounds
Since ˜K is finite, we may equally well denote it FQ. We must bound |BP (FQ)|. We decompose
BP into its absolutely irreducible components over ¯FQ:
BP =
m
[
i=1
Γi
where each Γi is an absolutely irreducible projective curve of degree di. Note that Pm
i=1 di = 9.
Before applying point-counting bounds, we note a technical detail regarding the field of defini-
tion. If an absolutely irreducible component Γi is not defined over FQ, its FQ-rational points must
lie in the intersection of Γi with its Frobenius conjugates. By B´ezout’s theorem, this intersection is
finite and yields at most d2
i ≤81 points. Since 81 = O(1), this is safely and strictly bounded above
by the Hasse-Weil bound for singular curves (Theorem 3.3) for sufficiently large Q.
For the components defined over FQ, we bound the number of rational points |Γi(FQ)| by
considering two cases:
10


--- Page 11 ---
• Case A: Γi is Smooth. If a component Γi is non-singular, we apply the standard Hasse-
Weil Bound. Let gi be the geometric genus of Γi.
||Γi(FQ)| −(Q + 1)| ≤2gi
p
Q
Thus,
|Γi(FQ)| ≤Q + 1 + 2gi
p
Q
• Case B: Γi is Singular. If a component Γi is singular, the standard Hasse-Weil bound does
not directly apply to the singular model. Let ˜Γi be the normalization (smooth model) of Γi.
There is a birational map ν : ˜Γi →Γi. The map is an isomorphism away from the singular
points of Γi. The number of singular points is bounded by the arithmetic genus. Crucially,
the number of rational points on the singular curve is bounded by the rational points on the
normalization plus the contribution from singularities (which is a small constant bound).
Theorem 3.3 unifies both cases using the arithmetic genus πi (which equals the geometric genus
for smooth curves and is strictly larger for singular curves).
For a curve of degree di in Pn, the arithmetic genus is sharply bounded above by the plane
projection bound: πi ≤(di−1)(di−2)
2
.
Step 3: Aggregating the Bounds
Summing over all components (both smooth and singular), and using the Hasse-Weil bound for
singular curves as a strict upper bound for any components not defined over FQ):
|BP (FQ)| ≤
m
X
i=1
|Γi(FQ)|
≤
m
X
i=1

Q + 1 + 2πi
p
Q

= mQ + m + 2
p
Q
m
X
i=1
πi
The number of components m is bounded by the total degree deg(BP ) = 9.
Since the plane
projection bound is superadditive (f(di + dj) ≥f(di) + f(dj)), the sum of arithmetic genera is
maximized when the degree is concentrated in a single irreducible component (m = 1, d1 = 9):
m
X
i=1
πi ≤
m
X
i=1
f(di) ≤f
 m
X
i=1
di
!
= (9 −1)(9 −2)
2
= 28.
Substituting these global bounds yields:
|BP (FQ)| ≤9Q + 56
p
Q + 9
This inequality relies only on the total degree and is completely uniform. It holds regardless of the
choice of point P and of whether the components are smooth or singular.
Step 4: Asymptotic Proof for n points
We compare the size of the combined bad locus to the total number of points on the surface.
11


--- Page 12 ---
• Surface: By the Weil Conjectures, the number of points on a smooth cubic surface over FQ
is Q2 +QTr(F|H2)+1, with the trace bounded below by −2 [Man86, Table 1], giving a strict,
universal lower bound on the total number of points: |˜V (FQ)| ≥Q2 −2Q + 1.
• Combined Bad Locus: For any set of n points {P1, . . . , Pn}, the set of points failing to be
in general position with at least one Pj is bounded by the union of their individual bad loci.
Thus,
 Sn
j=1 BPj(FQ)
 ≤n(9Q + 56√Q + 9).
We define the set of “General Position Candidate” for the set of n points as G = ˜V (FQ) \
Sn
j=1 BPj(FQ). Its size is bounded below by:
|G| ≥(Q2 −2Q + 1) −n(9Q + 56
p
Q + 9)
Consider the tower of quadratic extensions where Qj = |˜k|2j. As j →∞, Qj →∞. Clearly, for
sufficiently large Qj:
Q2
j > (2 + 9n)Qj + 56n
p
Qj + 9n −1
Because n and the constants are fixed, the Q2
j term strictly dominates. The threshold for Qj being
”sufficiently large” depends strictly on n and is absolutely uniform for any choice of n points.
Thus, by passing high enough up the tower of quadratic extensions (determined strictly by n),
we guarantee |G| > 0. Therefore, there exists at least one point P ′ ∈˜V ( ˜K) such that P ′ is in
general position with all n points Pj simultaneously.
This completes the proof of Theorem 3.4.
Lemma 3.5. Let i ∈{2, 3}. Suppose every point on ˜V (˜k) is i-class free. Then there is a bijection
between the equivalence classes V (k)/Ui and the equivalence classes ˜V (˜k)/ ˜Ui.
Proof. We will construct well-defined, mutually inverse maps between the sets of equivalence classes
V (k)/Ui and ˜V (˜k)/ ˜Ui.
Step 1: Pushing Ui down to ˜V (˜k)
Because every point ˜P ∈˜V (˜k) is i-class free, all lifts P ∈π−1( ˜P) belong to the exact same
Ui-class. We may thus define a map f : ˜V (˜k) →V (k)/Ui by setting f( ˜P) = [P]Ui, where P ∈V (k)
is any lift of ˜P.
We define an equivalence relation ∼α on ˜V (˜k) by:
˜A ∼α ˜B ⇐⇒f( ˜A) = f( ˜B).
We claim ∼α is an admissible equivalence on ˜V (˜k). We verify this by showing it satisfies the binary
operation rules described in Fact 2.3
• Secants: Let ˜P1 ̸= ˜P2 with no line on ˜V through them. They define a secant line ˜L intersecting
˜V at a third point ˜P3. By Lemma 2.16 (Swinnerton-Dyer), we can lift the collinear triple
( ˜P1, ˜P2, ˜P3) on ˜L ∩˜V to a collinear triple (P1, P2, P3) on a lifted line L. Since Ui is admissible
on V (k), [P3]Ui = [P1]Ui ◦[P2]Ui. Thus, the ∼α-class of ˜P3 is uniquely determined by the
∼α-classes of ˜P1 and ˜P2.
• Lines on ˜V : If ˜P1, ˜P2 lie on a line ˜L belonging to ˜V , Theorem 3.1 states that ˜L lifts to a
k-rational line L belonging to V . The admissibility of Ui forces L ∩V (k) to collapse into
a single Ui-class. Consequently, all points on ˜L map under f to this single class, effectively
collapsing ˜L into a single ∼α-class.
12


--- Page 13 ---
• Tangent Sections: If ˜P1 = ˜P2, consider the tangent section ˜C = ˜V ∩T ˜P1 ˜V . This configuration
lifts to a tangent section C = V ∩TP1V at a lifted point P1. By the admissibility of Ui, all
points on C \ {P1} share the same Ui-class. Their reductions onto ˜C \ { ˜P1} therefore map to
the same class under f, and are thus ∼α-equivalent.
Furthermore, ∼α explicitly inherits the i-th algebraic property from Ui via the map f:
• If i = 3: For any class [ ˜A]∼α, the operation [ ˜A]∼α ◦[ ˜A]∼α corresponds to f( ˜A) ◦f( ˜A) =
[A]U3 ◦[A]U3 = [A]U3 = f( ˜A). Thus, [ ˜A]∼α ◦[ ˜A]∼α = [ ˜A]∼α.
• If i = 2: For any class [ ˜A]∼α, the operation [ ˜A]∼α ◦[ ˜A]∼α corresponds to f( ˜A) ◦f( ˜A) =
[A]U2 ◦[A]U2 = X0. Thus, there is a fixed class ˜X0 ∈˜V (˜k)/ ∼α such that [ ˜A]∼α ◦[ ˜A]∼α = ˜X0.
Since ∼α is an admissible equivalence satisfying Property i, and ˜Ui is defined as the finest admissible
equivalence satisfying Property i on ˜V (˜k), ˜Ui must refine ∼α:
˜A ∼˜Ui ˜B =⇒
˜A ∼α ˜B =⇒f( ˜A) = f( ˜B) =⇒[A]Ui = [B]Ui.
This yields a well-defined mapping Φ : ˜V (˜k)/ ˜Ui →V (k)/Ui given by Φ([ ˜P] ˜Ui) = [P]Ui.
Step 2: Pulling ˜Ui up to V (k)
Conversely, we define an equivalence relation ∼β on V (k) via the reduction map:
A ∼β B ⇐⇒
˜A ∼˜Ui ˜B.
We verify that ∼β is an admissible equivalence on V (k).
Let (A1, A2, A3) and (B1, B2, B3) be
collinear triples in V (k) such that A1 ∼β B1 and A2 ∼β B2. Because the reduction of a line in P3
k is
a line in P3
˜k, and intersection multiplicities are preserved due to the smoothness of ˜V , the reductions
( ˜A1, ˜A2, ˜A3) and ( ˜B1, ˜B2, ˜B3) are collinear triples in ˜V (˜k). Since ˜Ui is an admissible equivalence, it
preserves collinearity, which implies ˜A3 ∼˜Ui ˜B3. This immediately gives A3 ∼β B3. Thus, ∼β is an
admissible equivalence on V (k).
Moreover, ∼β inherits the respective i-th algebraic property from ˜Ui:
• If i = 3: The operation A◦A modulo ∼β traces to ˜A◦˜A under ˜U3. By definition, [ ˜A◦˜A] ˜U3 =
[ ˜A] ˜U3, so A ◦A ∼β A.
• If i = 2: The relation dictates [ ˜A ◦˜A] ˜U2 = ˜X0. Pick any point in the class ˜X0 and let X0 be
a lift of that point. Then for all A ∈V (k), we have A ◦A ∼β X0.
Because Ui is the finest admissible equivalence on V (k) satisfying Property i, Ui must refine ∼β:
A ∼Ui B =⇒A ∼β B =⇒
˜A ∼˜Ui ˜B.
This yields a well-defined mapping Ψ : V (k)/Ui →˜V (˜k)/ ˜Ui given by Ψ([P]Ui) = [ ˜P] ˜Ui.
Step 3: Establishing the Bijection
The maps Φ and Ψ are mutually inverse:
• For any [P]Ui ∈V (k)/Ui, we have Φ(Ψ([P]Ui)) = Φ([ ˜P] ˜Ui) = [P ′]Ui, where P ′ is some lift of
˜P. Because ˜P is i-class free, all of its lifts belong to the same Ui-class, so [P ′]Ui = [P]Ui.
13


--- Page 14 ---
• For any [ ˜P] ˜Ui ∈˜V (˜k)/ ˜Ui, we have Ψ(Φ([ ˜P] ˜Ui)) = Ψ([P]Ui) = [ ˜P] ˜Ui.
Therefore, Φ and Ψ form a strict bijection between V (k)/Ui and ˜V (˜k)/ ˜Ui.
Now, we prove Theorem A:
Theorem A. Let V be a smooth cubic surface over a 2-adic local field k with good reduction such
that points ˜V (˜k) ̸= ∅are all Eckardt. Then R-equivalence on V (k) is trivial or of exponent 2.
Proof. Suppose there exists a tower of quadratic extensions, ˜K/˜k, such that the reduced surface
˜V ( ˜K) contains a rational line. Let K/k be the corresponding tower of quadratic extensions. Then
by Theorem 3.1 this line lifts, and via Theorem 3.2, universal equivalence over the extension is
3-torsion-free trivial (MU(K)[3] ∼= 1).
Else, suppose not. Then by [SD81, §5] the original reduction ˜V (˜k) is isomorphic to one of three
line-free cases, which we abbreviate as n = 1, n = 3, and n = 9. For the case n = 1, recall it is the
unique case of an all-Eckardt reduction on which ˜H∗does not identically vanish (see [SD81, §1]).
Hence, any finite extension K is no longer an exception to Theorem 2.15 and therefore V (K) is
trivial for any finite K over k.
Next, note that the number of Eckardt points on smooth cubic surfaces is at most 45 in any
characteristic (e.g., [Hir85, Lemma 20.2.7]). By passing to the tower of quadratic extensions K over
k given by Theorem 3.4 for n = 2, the reduced surface ˜V ( ˜K) acquires non-Eckardt points while
still containing no lines. Because the original reduction ˜V (˜k) consisted entirely of Eckardt points,
we may choose one such point ˜E ∈˜V (˜k). Over ˜K, the point ˜E is still Eckardt and by Theorem 3.4
is in general position with some other point. Therefore, by Theorem 2.21, the universal equivalence
classes of the lifts of ˜E form a CML of period 2. Consequently, the 3-torsion among its lifts is
trivial, meaning ˜E is 3-class-free. (Note this relies strictly on a p-adic volumetric argument and
does not require ˜E to lift to a rational Eckardt point in V (K)).
We now propagate this property to the entire surface. First, recall the secant lifting property
[SD81, remark before Lemma 15]: if a point ˜A is 3-class-free, and is in general position with ˜B
(yielding a third distinct transversal intersection ˜C), we may fix a single lift C ∈V (K) of ˜C. Then
for any lift B of ˜B, the line BC intersects V at a third point AB lifting ˜A. Because ˜A is 3-class-free,
[AB]U3 is a constant class. Thus [B]U3 = [AB]U3 ◦[C]U3 is also constant, making ˜B 3-class-free.
Therefore, to prove a point is 3-class-free, it suffices to find a single 3-class-free point in general
position with it.
Let ˜P ∈˜V ( ˜K) be an arbitrary point. Applying Theorem 3.4 to the pair of points { ˜E, ˜P},
there exists a point ˜Q ∈˜V ( ˜K) that is in general position with both ˜E and ˜P simultaneously.
Because ˜Q is in general position with ˜E, the point ˜Q becomes 3-class-free. Because ˜P is in general
position with ˜Q, the point ˜P also becomes 3-class-free. Since ˜P was arbitrary, every single point
on ˜V ( ˜K) is 3-class-free. This fulfills the strict hypothesis of Theorem 3.5, providing the bijection
V (K)/U3 ∼= ˜V ( ˜K)/ ˜U3. Since ˜V ( ˜K) contains no lines, ˜V ( ˜K)/ ˜U3 is trivial by Theorem 2.14.∗Hence,
we have a tower of quadratic extensions K over k such that #{V (K)/U3} consists of one element.
∗A brief sketch of a possible proof first appeared in the preprint: D. Kanevsky, “Some remarks on Brauer equiv-
alence for cubic surfaces,” Max Planck Inst. f¨ur Math. (1984). While other results from that preprint were cited in
Manin’s Cubic Forms, this specific problem was not included among the solved cases discussed therein. We believe
that the absence of formal proofs regarding the existence of general position upon extension, as well as the bijection
of 3-universal equivalence classes under modulo 2 reduction, constituted significant gaps. Consequently, the problem
has effectively remained open until now.
14


--- Page 15 ---
Using Theorem 2.18 we get
#MR(k)[3] ≤#MU(K)[3] = 1,
(4)
i.e., the 3-torsion component of MR(k)[3] (R-equivalence over the base local field), consists of one
element. Then by the structure theorem for R-equivalence CMLs (Theorem 2.11), MR(k) is trivial
or has exponent 2.
4
Proof of Theorem B
In this section we will prove Theorem B:
Theorem B. Let V defined by Equation (2) be smooth such that ˜V has exactly one point. Then
V (Q2) constitutes a single class of R-equivalence and two classes of universal equivalence.
Our proof consists of three parts. In the first part, following Section 3.4 in [Kan82], we demon-
strate the existence of an admissible equivalence on this surface consisting of exactly two classes. In
the second part, we show that any point on a transformed surface possessing two irrational tangent
lines in its reduction modulo 2 is class-free. This immediately implies that the remaining points
on the tangent curve—defined by the intersection of the tangent plane and the transformed cubic
surface modulo 2—are also class-free. Finally, we develop a novel R2-equivalence method and prove
that it is trivial.
4.1
The Surface with One-Point Reduction
First, one can confirm that the reduction of Equation (2) is isomorphic to the “unique” single-point
reduced surface in [SD81, 5.5].
We begin as in [Kan82, §3.1] by transforming Equation (2) from F(T0, T1, T2, T3) into F1(T0, T ′
1, T ′
2, T ′
3)
via a change of variables, followed by division by the factor 23:
Φ1 : T0 →T0,
T1 →23T ′
1,
T2 →2T ′
2,
T3 →2T ′
3
(5)
The transformed polynomial F1 is obtained by dividing the sum of the expanded terms by the
factor 23. We group the resulting terms by the remaining power of 2.
F1(T0, T ′
1, T ′
2, T ′
3) = F(T0, 23T ′
1, 2T ′
2, 2T ′
3)
23
= T 2
0 T ′
1 + T0(b0T ′2
2 + b1T ′
2T ′
3 + b2T ′2
3 ) +
␀(T ′
2)3 + (T ′
2)2T ′
3 + (T ′
3)3
+ 2(terms in T0, T ′
1, T ′
2, T ′
3)
(6)
This equation defines a projective cubic surface
V1 : F1(T0, T ′
1, T ′
2, T ′
3) = 0
(7)
such that modulo 2 it defines the cubic surface
˜V1 : ˜T 2
0 ˜T ′
1 + ˜T0( ˜T ′2
2 + ˜T ′
2 ˜T ′
3 + ˜T ′2
3 ) + ( ˜T ′
2)3 + ( ˜T ′
2)2 ˜T ′
3 + ( ˜T ′
3)3 = 0.
(8)
Next, we define a partition of the set ˜S ⊂˜V1(F2) based on the value of the quadratic form
Q(y, z) = y2 + yz + z2.
15


--- Page 16 ---
• Class ˜X0: Points where the quadratic form vanishes (Q = 0):
˜X0 = { ˜P ∈˜S | Q( ˜T ′
2, ˜T ′
3) = 0} = {(1, 0, 0, 0)},
(9)
• Class ˜X1: Points where the quadratic form is non-zero (Q = 1):
˜X1 = { ˜P ∈˜S | Q( ˜T ′
2, ˜T ′
3) = 1} = {(1, 0, 1, 0), (1, 0, 1, 1), (1, 0, 0, 1)}.
(10)
We define an equivalence relation A on the original V (k) by lifting these classes.
Two points
P, Q ∈V (k) are A-equivalent if their reductions (after the transformation Φ1) fall into the same
set Xi. We denote as class Xi ⊂V1(k) the a set of points on V1(k) lying above ˜Xi. One verifies
that ˜X0, ˜X1 are the universal equivalence classes of the reduced surface such that ˜X0 ◦˜X0 = ˜X1
and ˜X1 ◦˜X1 = ˜X1. Pulling back to the surface V over 2-adic k, [Kan82] showed that X0, X1 define
a period-2 component in the universal equivalence of V (k).
Next, we observe that per Theorem A, we know R-equivalence on V (k) is 3-torsion-free. Now,
we will prove that R-equivalence on V (k) is 2-torsion-free in the next subsection.
4.2
Finishing Proof of Theorem B
Lemma 4.1. Let ˜W be the projective cubic surface over F2 defined by the homogeneous equation:
˜G(X, Y, Z, T) = X2T + X(Y 2 + Y Z + Z2) + Y 3 + Y 2Z + Z3 = 0.
(11)
Let ˜P0 = (1 : 0 : 1 : 0) and let ˜S be the locus of points Q ∈˜W(F2) such that ˜P0 lies on the tangent
plane Π(Q). Then the point ˜P = (1 : 0 : 0 : 0) is a non-singular point of ˜S.
Proof. We follow the method in Lemma 16 of [SD81]. Take coordinates such that ˜P = (1, 0, 0, 0),
˜P0 = (0, 1, 0, 0) and Π( ˜P) is T = 0. Then the equation of ˜W is
f = X2T + terms at most linear in X = 0
(12)
and f has no terms in Y 3. The curve ˜S has equation FY = 0 where the subscript denotes differen-
tiation. ˜S has singular point at ˜P if and only if
fXY = fY Y = fY Z = 0 at ˜P
(13)
Since char F2 = 2 this means that ˜P is singular on ˜S if f has no term in XY Z, i.e. ˜P is a cusp on
Γ( ˜P). But one can immediately check that ˜P = (1, 0, 0, 0) is not a cusp.
Lemma 4.2. Let W be the cubic surface over Q2 defined by
˜G(X, Y, Z, T) = X2T + X(Y 2 + Y Z + Z2) + Y 3 + Y 2Z + Z3 + 2(...) = 0.
(14)
Then ˜P = (1, 0, 0, 0) ∈˜W(F2) is class free.
Proof. We follow the method in Lemma 15 and Lemma 17 of [SD81].
Step 1:
Let P = (1, 0, 0, 0) and P ′ = (1, y, z, t) be distinct points of W above ˜P. The intersection of
the two tangent planes Π(P) ∩Π(P ′) within T = 0 yields a line L that modulo 2 belongs to the
16


--- Page 17 ---
tangent plane at ˜P. It can be shown as in the following. Because P ′ is close to P in the 2-adic
topology, substituting the partial derivatives of G to the first order in p-adic valuations shows this
line is governed by:
Y GY (P ′) + ZGZ(P ′) = 0
(15)
Evaluating the formal partial derivatives, we find GY (P ′) ≈z and F1Z(P ′) ≈y modulo higher-
order terms. By symmetry, we may assume v(y) ≥v(z). We have v(t) > v(z). The intersection line
L thus takes the form Y + (y/z)Z + · · · = 0, i.e. the line L that modulo 2 belongs to the tangent
plane at ˜P. Hence ˜L mod 2 meets ˜W again in a rational point ˜P1 ̸= ˜P2, and so L meets W in a
rational point P1 above ˜P1.
Step 2:
Let C be the class of P1. Since all rational points of Γ(P) except perhaps P belong to the same
class, all rational points of Γ(P) and Γ(P ′) except perhaps P and P ′ belong to C. Let P2 be any
rational point on W such that ˜P2 = (1, 0, 1, 0) ∈Γ( ˜P). By Theorem 4.1 there exists a rational P ′
on W above ˜P such that P2 lies on Γ(P ′); hence P2 ∈C.
Now let L1 denote the line PP ′.
A calculation like in Step 1 shows that ˜L1 is a rational line
through ˜P in Π( ˜P). Therefore, as for L above, L1 meets W again in a rational point P2 of the kind
described above. Now P, P, P1 and P, P ′, P2 are collinear. But P1 ∼P2. Therefore P ∼P ′.
4.2.1
R-equivalence for Cubics Reducing to One Point
To prove the R-equivalence part of Theorem B we need the following statement:
Lemma 4.3. Let V be a cubic surface over a local p-adic field k. Let P ∈V (k) be a point such
that its reduction ˜P = P (mod p) is a smooth point on ˜V = V (mod p), and ˜V contains no lines
passing through ˜P.
Let K be a separable quadratic extension of k. Let {Qi}i≥1 be a sequence of points in V (K)\V (k)
converging to P in the p-adic topology. Let Q′
i be the Galois conjugate of Qi over k. Let ri be the
third point of intersection of the line Li passing through Qi and Q′
i with V .
Then the sequence of lines Li converges to a tangent line to V at P defined over k, and the
sequence of points ri converges to the intersection of this tangent line with V (specifically to the
point R such that the intersection cycle is 2P + R).
Proof. We proceed in steps:
Step 1: Local Coordinates and Smoothness
Since ˜P is a smooth point of ˜V , P is a smooth point of V (by Hensel’s Lemma/lifting of the
non-vanishing gradient). We can choose affine coordinates (x, y, z) defined over the ring of integers
of k such that P is the origin (0, 0, 0) and the tangent plane TP V is given by z = 0. The equation
of the cubic surface V can be written as:
F(x, y, z) = F1(x, y, z) + F2(x, y, z) + F3(x, y, z) = 0
where F1, F2, F3 are homogeneous polynomials of degrees 1, 2, and 3 respectively. Since z = 0 is
the tangent plane, F1(x, y, z) = z (up to a unit scaling factor). Thus:
F(x, y, z) = z + F2(x, y, z) + F3(x, y, z) = 0
Step 2: Parametrization of Points
17


--- Page 18 ---
Let K = k(
√
D) for some D ∈k \ k2. Since Qi ∈V (K) \ V (k) and Qi →P, we can write Qi in
terms of its coordinates in the basis {1,
√
D}:
Qi = Ai +
√
DBi
where Ai, Bi ∈k × k × k. The Galois conjugate is Q′
i = Ai −
√
DBi. Since Qi →P = (0, 0, 0), we
have Ai →0 and Bi →0 in the p-adic topology. Since Qi /∈V (k), Qi ̸= Q′
i, which implies Bi ̸= 0.
Step 3: Convergence to a Tangent Line
The line Li passing through Qi and Q′
i is parameterized by R(t) = Ai + tBi. The direction of
this line is given by the vector Bi. Since Qi lies on V , F(Qi) = 0. Using the Taylor expansion of
F around Ai:
F(Ai +
√
DBi) = F(Ai) + ∇F(Ai) · (
√
DBi) + O(|Bi|2) = 0
Considering the terms associated with
√
D (the irrational part), and dividing by
√
D (since Bi ̸= 0):
∇F(Ai) · Bi + Higher Order Terms = 0
As i →∞, Ai →P. By the continuity of the gradient, ∇F(Ai) →∇F(P). Since Bi →0, the
higher order terms vanish faster than linear terms. Thus, the direction vectors vi = Bi/∥Bi∥satisfy:
lim
i→∞∇F(P) · vi = 0
This implies that any limit direction of the secant lines lies in the kernel of the gradient form at P,
which is exactly the tangent plane TP V . Thus, the line Li converges to a line L∞passing through
P and contained in the tangent plane TP V . Since Li passes through k-rational points Ai with
k-rational direction Bi, the limit line is defined over k.
Step 4: The Third Intersection Point
The intersection of the line Li with V is determined by the roots of the cubic polynomial
gi(t) = F(Ai + tBi). The roots are t1 =
√
D (corresponding to Qi) and t2 = −
√
D (corresponding
to Q′
i). Let t3 be the parameter for the third point ri. By Vieta’s formulas, the sum of the roots
satisfies t1 + t2 + t3 = −coeff of t2
coeff of t3 .
√
D −
√
D + t3 = t3 = −F2(i)
F3(i)
where C3(i) is the coefficient of the cubic term of F restricted to Li. In the limit, the line becomes
a tangent line L∞.
The condition that “there is no line on ˜V through ˜P” guarantees that the restriction of the cubic
form to the tangent plane is not identically zero, and specifically, the reduced tangent line is not
contained in ˜V . By Hensel’s Lemma structures, this implies L∞is not contained in V . Thus, the
intersection cycle L∞· V is a finite 0-cycle of degree 3. Since L∞is tangent at P, the intersection
cycle is of the form 2P + R, where R ∈V (k). By the continuity of the roots of polynomials with
respect to their coefficients, the point ri converges to R.
Since L∞⊂TP V , the point R lies on the intersection curve CP = V ∩TP V . Therefore, ri
converges to a point on the tangent plane defined over k.
18


--- Page 19 ---
With the lemmas established, we proceed to prove the R-equivalence part of B. Let U denote the
universal equivalence relation on V (k). The set of equivalence classes V (k)/U consists of two classes,
denoted X0, X1 ⊂V (Q2), which satisfy the composition laws X0 ◦X0 = X1 and X1 ◦X1 = X1
under the collinear binary operation on the cubic surface.
Proof of R-equivalence in the one-point case. Consider the unramified quadratic extension K =
Q2(θ), where θ2 + θ + 1 = 0. The points Z1 = (1, θ, 0, 0) and Z2 = (1, θ2, 0, 0) in V (K) are Galois
conjugate, are in general position, and are not Eckardt points. Consequently, by Theorem 2.15, all
points in V (K) are universally equivalent.
Let P1 be a point in X1 and let P0 be any point in X0. We will prove that P0 ∼P1 (mod R).
Consider the reduction modulo 2. The set X0 (mod 2) consists of a single point in ˜V (F2),
which we denote by ˜P0. Let ˜Z1 = Z1 (mod 2) and ˜Z2 = Z2 (mod 2). These are conjugate points
in general position on ˜V ( ˜K), and the triple ˜P0, ˜Z1, ˜Z2 is collinear on ˜V .
For any point P0 ∈X0 ⊂V (Q2) lying above ˜P0, we can choose a line L defined over Q2 passing
through P0 such that its reduction ˜L = L (mod 2) passes through ˜Z1 and ˜Z2. Let the intersection
of L with V (K) be the cycle P0 + H1 + H2, where H1, H2 ∈V (K). The points H1 and H2 are
conjugate over Q2 and are in general position.
Since V (K) consists of a single universal equivalence class, there exists a chain of rational curves
connecting H1 to P1 (viewed as a point in V (K)). We follow the method of Manin described in
[Man86, Section 15.1.3]. There exists a sequence of points x0, x1, . . . , xr+1 in V (K) with x0 = H1
and xr+1 = P1, such that these points are connected by K-morphisms
fi : P1
K →V ⊗K,
with fi(0) = xi and fi(∞) = xi+1.
According to [Man86, §15.1.3.iii], the intermediate points x1, . . . , xr−1 can be chosen such that the
conjugate pairs xi, ¯xi in V (K) are in general position for all i = 1, . . . , r −1.
We let the non-trivial automorphism of the extension K/Q2 act on P1
K and V ⊗K via the
second factor. This action defines the conjugate morphisms ¯fi. We define the trace morphisms
gi = fi ◦¯fi : P1
Q2 →V,
for i = 0, . . . , r.
By definition, for all t ∈P1(Q2), the point gi(t) is the third intersection point of the line connecting
fi(t) and ¯fi(t) with the surface V . In particular,
gi(0) = xi ◦¯xi,
gi(∞) = xi+1 ◦¯xi+1.
Since xr+1 = P1 ∈V (Q2), we analyze the final morphism gr. It is a rational map from P1(Q2) to
V (Q2), defined as a morphism on a Zariski open set U ⊂P1(Q2).
In the 2-adic topology, we can find points in U sufficiently close to ∞∈P1
Q2 such that their
images P ′ under gr are close to Q′ ∈X1. Consequently (from Theorem 4.3), for some neighborhood
W ′ of ∞in P1
Q2 under the 2-adic topology, the images of all points P ′ in W under gr map into the
class X1. This implies that the rational curve defined by gr connects the component containing
xr ◦¯xr to a point in X1.
Tracing back through the chain (starting from g0(0) = H1 ◦¯H1 = P0), we conclude that P0 is
R-equivalent to a point in X1. Since P0 was arbitrary, this proves the theorem.
19


--- Page 20 ---
5
Disclosure of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Use
Our explorations began in March 2025, and so many of our interactions predate suggested norms on
cataloging and presenting AI use (e.g., [Sch25, FTB+26]). A further complication that our is process
is many-to-many with our presentation; that is, our publications derive from many overlapping
interactions, with release order modulated primarily by human pedagogy, taste, and confidence
in results. Hence, we defer interaction specifics (e.g., human-AI interaction cards [FTB+26]) and
overall lessons learned to a unified companion report [SKH+26], where we frame this paper as one
of many within our AI-assisted effort to fill out the theory of admissible equivalences on cubic
surfaces.
Exposition and definitions are mostly human-written. Proofs of new lemmas are largely written
by large language models (LLMs), Gemini 3 Pro and Deep Think, but under human direction and
editing. Specifically:
• In understanding why [Man86] did not mention the claimed proof of the unpublished preprint
[Kan84] despite referencing its other results, we identified the assumptions of sufficient points
in general position and the bijection of equivalence classes upon lifting as critical gaps. We
conceived that both could be closed with standard methods, but the exact methods and their
rigorous use (Theorems 3.4 and 3.5) were done by Gemini 3 Deep Think.
• Theorem 4.1 and Theorem 4.2 are based on [SD81, Lemma 15-17]. They were respectively
verified by Gemini 3 Pro and drafted by Gemini 3 Deep Think.
• Theorem 4.3 was written by Gemini 3 Deep Think with manual guidance.
The authors
would/could not have made this proof rigorous to the level attained by AI.
Below is the timeline of interactions resulting in this work. Note that the extended period does
not necessarily reflect the intrinsic effort required or capability of named models, as (i) this work is
a secondary project by AI researchers at Google DeepMind who happened to have mathematical
training a decade ago, and (ii) this is only the first paper derived from the time period:
• March 2025: Our investigation began with our interest in finding the R-equivalence of the 3-
adic version of Manin’s surface, i.e., over Q3(θ) (bad reduction). The non-associative universal
equivalence on this surface was only recently established by [Kan23, Kan25]. The authors felt
that AI tools had matured sufficiently to be useful for this problem. (In a follow-up work we
will show that R-equivalence is also equal to Brauer equivalence in this case.)
• April 2025: For LLM use, we acquired and digitized copies of works such as [Kan82] and
[SD81, §8], who gave a construction (a curve given by intersecting a cubic and quadric)
demonstrating trivial R-equivalence over F2(θ). Under incorrect intuition that this construc-
tion lifted to Q2(θ), we wanted to find a similar construction over F3(θ) that lifted to Q3(θ).
We used AlphaEvolve [NVE+25] to automate the search for valid intersections with the right
genus, which worked but quickly plateaued, leading us to reconsider our assumption. Gemini
2.5 Pro [CBS+25] devised an argument for why the characteristic 2 construction could not
lift as-is. (In Feb. 2026 we would finally get a copy of [SD01], who admitted the same!)
• May 2025: This failure is consistent with the statement of Manin’s, that we find at this
point, in the final edition of Cubic Forms [Man86]—suggesting the 2-adic problem was still
20


--- Page 21 ---
open. Rapid ideation and discussions with Gemini 2.5 Pro commenced, culminating in a
natural language proof written by Gemini. We were excited to share what we believed to be
the first research mathematics result co-written by generative AI.
• August 2025: Gemini 2.5 Pro with web search rediscovered the first author’s preprint
[Kan84] on a Max Planck Institute web server, which gave a similar approach (in intuitive
detail) to Manin’s question. Due to its age (40+ years ago), even the first author no longer
had a copy of their work and forgot their own “proof!” We paused our announcement.
• Sept. to Nov. 2025: We grew to understand why Manin (despite citing [Kan84] in [Man86]
for other results) and later [SD01] might not have considered the problem solved; see footnote
to the proof of Theorem A in Section 3. Gemini Deep Think and Gemini 3 Pro (preview)
are released; due to their improved capabilities, we performed deeper scrutiny and requested
levels of detail unusual for publications in algebraic geometry. Critiquing these revived our
mathematical training, improved our knowledge, and gave new prompting and proof strate-
gies. Reports of LLM progress on research math proliferate; see [FTB+26, §7] for a survey.
• Dec. 2025 to Feb. 2026: Using Gemini 3 Pro and Deep Think, we built atop our initial
insights from Gemini 2.5 Pro and used them to write highly detailed proofs closing the gaps
in [Kan84]’s argument and thus resolving Manin’s 1972 question. These LLMs also realized
a program to extend our work to the non-trivial universal equivalence case in [Kan82, §3.4];
their large casework and mixed success led the authors to simpler geometric reasoning whose
lemmas AI made rigorous. Together, we viewed these as a coherent set of results for release.
• Mar. 2026: Final writing refinements aided by Gemini 3.1 Pro and Deep Think, improving
presentation correctness (e.g., for Theorem 3.3, citing [AP96] [LY94] instead of a later Aubry-
Perret work that restates/refines the bounds) and streamlining exposition (e.g., using [KS03]
to avoid “general type” restrictions, or skipping quasigroups along the way to defining CMLs).
We share this timeline to give a human and historical angle to our work. Due to the ambiguity
about how “essential” AI was in our situation, and as the final proof approaches were human-
driven (despite some initial versions by AI), we follow the guidance of [FTB+26, 5.1.1] and class
this publication-level human-AI work as “primarily human” (H2) in their taxonomy. That said, we
believe our process demonstrates a sustained non-trivial human-AI collaboration where AI’s role
continues to increase, as we find in forthcoming C2-level works in this series.
